topic: Harry Potter, as an Auror, fighting against with a formidable enemy in year 2026. Document describes the background, rivalry, details, investigations and actions done by ministry and potter.
document_text:
## Section 1: The Return of an Auror's Vigilance

In the autumn of 2026, Senior Auror Harry Potter found himself at the centre of the most consequential investigation of his post-war career. Now forty-six years old, Potter had served the Auror Office for more than two decades, ascending to the rank of Senior Auror in 2009 and refusing repeated promotion offers that would have placed him behind a desk. His preference for fieldwork had become legendary within the Department of Magical Law Enforcement, and his recent assignment to the Vorlund Case was widely regarded as both a vote of confidence and an acknowledgment that no other Auror possessed the necessary combination of experience and personal resolve.

The Vorlund Case took its name from Malachar Vorlund, a wizard whose emergence over the previous three years had quietly disturbed the Ministry of Magic in ways not seen since the Second Wizarding War. Potter had first encountered the name in a routine intelligence briefing in March 2024, when reports of unauthorised magical experimentation in the Yorkshire Dales had crossed his desk. By the spring of 2025, those reports had transformed from curiosity into alarm. Vorlund's particular speciality—the manipulation of memory through ritual rather than charm—had begun appearing in cases as far afield as Aberdeen and Plymouth. Witnesses described a tall, ash-haired man in his mid-fifties who left behind no traceable wand signature, a detail that more than any other had convinced Potter that Vorlund was no ordinary criminal.

By July of 2026, the Auror Office had formally designated Malachar Vorlund as a Class A Magical Threat, the highest classification used since the apprehension of the final Death Eater holdouts in 2003. Potter's caseload was reorganised so that he could devote himself almost exclusively to the pursuit. He kept a personal dossier on Vorlund in the upper drawer of his desk in Office 47B, a small annex on the second floor of the Auror Department that he had requested for its quietness rather than its prestige. The dossier contained eighty-seven separate intelligence summaries, twenty-three witness statements, four partial photographs taken at distance, and a single fragment of parchment recovered from a raided safehouse in Inverness. That parchment, marked with a circular sigil resembling a stylised eclipse, had become the closest thing the Aurors had to a calling card for their quarry.

Potter was not the only investigator to have studied Vorlund, but he was the only one who had survived a direct encounter. In June 2026, during a tip-off pursuit through the abandoned Brackenford Mill in Cumbria, Potter had come within twenty paces of the dark wizard before a collapsing staircase—almost certainly enchanted to fall on cue—had separated them. In the brief moment before the dust obscured his vision, Potter had seen Vorlund smile, tip his head as though acknowledging a worthy opponent, and Disapparate with a deliberateness that bore no resemblance to flight. The encounter had left Potter with two cracked ribs and an abiding conviction that Vorlund was, for reasons not yet apparent, choosing to be hunted.

Within the Ministry, debate persisted over the nature of Vorlund's ambitions. Some senior officials, most notably the recently retired Auror Bertram Quill, had argued that Vorlund was simply the latest in a long line of opportunistic dark wizards whose threat had been overstated by media coverage and political convenience. Quill, who had spent three decades chasing minor figures in the dark artefact trade, was inclined to view all such investigations through the lens of his own experience and was not above suggesting that Vorlund was merely a particularly photogenic smuggler. Potter had publicly disagreed in a Wizengamot subcommittee hearing on the eighteenth of August, an exchange that the Daily Prophet had characterised, with its usual restraint, as a "blistering rebuke." The truth was less dramatic. Potter had simply observed that Vorlund's pattern of strikes—deliberately spaced, geographically scattered, and aimed at targets of negligible material value—suggested an intelligence and a purpose that no opportunist would possess.

The investigation had therefore begun in earnest. Potter requested and received expanded surveillance authority on the eleventh of September 2026. He drew on the resources of the Department of Magical Law Enforcement, the Hit Wizard reserves, and the small but capable Magical Reversal Squad. He had also begun, quietly and without official sanction, to consult with private parties whose knowledge of Vorlund's likely interests exceeded that of any Ministry archive. By the close of September, the Vorlund Case was no longer simply a pursuit. It had become, in the carefully chosen phrasing of one internal memo, "the defining engagement of the present Auror generation."

## Section 2: The Ministry in 2026

The Ministry of Magic in 2026 was, by most internal estimates, the most administratively sound it had been in living memory. Hermione Granger-Weasley had assumed the office of Minister for Magic in May 2024 following the unexpected resignation of her predecessor, Algernon Twycross, and her two years in the post had been marked by a programme of structural reform that had touched nearly every department. The Auror Office had been one of the principal beneficiaries. Granger-Weasley had increased its operating budget by seventeen percent, expanded its training intake by a third, and authorised the creation of a dedicated Threat Assessment Bureau under the umbrella of the Department of Magical Law Enforcement.

The Department of Magical Law Enforcement, headed since 2022 by the formidable Dame Cordelia Ashworth, had become the Ministry's largest department by both staff and expenditure. Its remit had expanded to include not only traditional Auror operations but also the regulation of magical artefacts, the supervision of international magical cooperation, and the oversight of the Wizengamot's investigative apparatus. Critics within the Wizarding press had occasionally complained that the Department had grown too large for any single director to manage effectively, a concern that Ashworth had addressed in her annual address by noting that "the alternative to sufficient resources is insufficient justice." Whatever one made of the rhetoric, the operational results were difficult to dispute. Reported incidents of dark magic had fallen by twelve percent in 2025, while successful prosecutions had risen by nearly nine.

Yet the Ministry's confidence in 2026 was tempered by an awareness that the apparent quietude of recent years was deceptive. Internal intelligence circulated among senior officials had warned, in increasingly direct terms, that several extremist factions were reorganising in the wake of the post-war settlement. Some of these factions—the loose remnants of the Greengrass Confederacy, the small but persistent Mortregor cells in continental Europe, and the disorganised followers of the now-deceased Vandram brothers—were considered manageable. Others were not. The Ministry's particular concern was the steady increase in the trade of artefacts whose magical lineage predated the founding of Hogwarts itself, a market that had existed in some form for centuries but which had grown markedly more active since 2023.

Granger-Weasley had spoken privately, on at least three documented occasions, about her belief that this artefact trade was being driven by "consolidation rather than consumer demand." She suspected that someone was systematically acquiring objects of significant magical antiquity, and she had ordered the Department of Magical Law Enforcement to coordinate with Gringotts on enhanced monitoring of vault transfers. The cooperation, while officially unprecedented, had yielded only modest intelligence. Goblin custodianship traditions made systematic surveillance impossible, and the Ministry's authority extended only to those vaults whose registered holders had given explicit consent. By the late summer of 2026, the artefact monitoring programme had identified seventeen suspicious transactions but had failed to determine the identity of the principal actor behind them.

The Auror Office's response to these broader concerns had been to restructure its caseload around what the new Threat Assessment Bureau termed "high-coherence threats"—criminal actors whose operations exhibited patterns consistent with long-term, organised intent. The classification was deliberately distinct from the older categories of "dark wizard," "rogue practitioner," or "extremist faction," which had been judged inadequate to describe the kinds of adversaries the Aurors increasingly faced. Senior Auror Harry Potter had been instrumental in drafting the new classification framework, and his Vorlund Case was the first to be processed under it. Other cases, less publicised, had also been reclassified. Among them were investigations into the disappearance of three artefact dealers in Bristol, a series of unexplained magical disturbances in the Hebrides, and the still-unsolved breach of a sealed chamber beneath the ruins of Carradoch Abbey in October 2025.

Within the Auror Office itself, morale was high but cautious. The intake of 2024 had produced an exceptionally talented cohort of junior Aurors, several of whom had distinguished themselves in field operations within months of qualifying. The Office's training programme, redesigned by Senior Instructor Elspeth Marrowbye, had introduced a new emphasis on counter-ritualistic magic—an explicit response to the kinds of threats that had begun to define the era. The older Aurors, those who had served during or shortly after the Second Wizarding War, had been encouraged to mentor the new arrivals, and the resulting transmission of practical experience had been one of the quieter successes of Granger-Weasley's reform programme.

It was within this institutional context that Potter pursued his investigation. He had every advantage that the modern Ministry could provide. What he lacked, and what the Ministry could not provide, was the certainty that the threat he pursued was a threat the Ministry had correctly understood. The Bureau's preliminary assessments had been clear that the Vorlund Case did not resemble any of the recognised templates of the post-war era, and that the pattern of his activity suggested an organisation rather than an individual—although the precise contours of that organisation remained, at the close of September, almost entirely unknown to the Ministry's most senior analysts.

## Section 3: The Origins of Malachar Vorlund

Malachar Vorlund had not always been the figure who haunted the Auror Office's most secure briefing rooms. He had been born in 1971 to the Vorlund family of Threlkeld in the Lake District, a household of moderate magical pedigree whose principal claim to historical interest was a distant connection to the seventeenth-century theorist Ezekiel Vorlund, author of the now-suppressed treatise Memory and Its Architectures. Malachar was the youngest of three siblings. His elder brother, Cassian, had emigrated to the Australian magical community in 1989 and had played no role in subsequent events. His sister, Therena, had died in 1994 in circumstances that the family had described, with a brevity that some investigators would later find significant, as "an unfortunate accident."

Malachar had attended Hogwarts between 1982 and 1989. He had been sorted into Ravenclaw, where he had distinguished himself principally in the disciplines of Arithmancy and Ancient Runes, and where he had earned a reputation among his teachers for an analytical intelligence that one report had described as "unusually cold for a child of his age." He had not been a notable presence in the Slug Club, in school politics, or in any of the social institutions that typically marked future Ministry careers. Upon leaving Hogwarts he had declined an offered position with the Department of Mysteries—a refusal that Department records noted with terse disappointment—and had instead departed for an extended period of independent study in Romania, Bulgaria, and what was then still the German Democratic Republic. He returned to Britain in 1996 and lived, by all available evidence, an unremarkable life as a private magical scholar until 2011.

What occurred in 2011 remained the subject of some dispute. The Auror Office's reconstruction, assembled chiefly from Vorlund's own writings recovered from a raided cottage in 2024, suggested that Vorlund had during this period made what he later described as his "first authentic contact" with a body of pre-modern magical doctrine that he believed had been deliberately suppressed by the institutional Wizarding establishment. He had begun, in 2012, to correspond with a small circle of like-minded scholars across Europe. He had begun, in 2014, to recruit. By 2018, he had founded the organisation that would consume the rest of his career and become the principal target of Harry Potter's investigation: The Obsidian Concord.

The Obsidian Concord, as Vorlund conceived it, was not a continuation of any earlier dark Wizarding movement. He had been explicit on this point in the few writings that had survived his subsequent operational caution. He had no interest in Voldemortian ideology, no patience for blood-purity politics, and no appetite for the theatrical violence that had characterised the Death Eater era. His ambition, insofar as it could be reconstructed, was instead a kind of magical restoration—a return to forms of practice that he believed had been deliberately erased by the consolidations of the high medieval period. The Obsidian Concord was named, according to one of Vorlund's recovered notebooks, after the "obsidian glass through which the older world might still be perceived." The naming had no connection, despite repeated press speculation, to the small obsidian-trading guild that had operated in fifteenth-century Venice.

This ideological distinctness was part of what made Vorlund so difficult to combat. The institutional knowledge of the Auror Office, accumulated through decades of fighting Voldemort and his successors, was poorly calibrated to a movement whose ambitions were neither political nor genocidal. Vorlund's followers did not march, did not issue manifestos, and did not commit the kinds of public atrocities that would have generated easy political consensus against them. They studied. They acquired. They occasionally killed, but only with what investigators had come to recognise as a chillingly utilitarian discrimination.

The Auror Office's understanding of the Concord's organisational structure remained incomplete in 2026. Vorlund himself was understood to be its principal authority, but the existence of an inner council of perhaps six to eight senior members had been inferred from intercepted correspondence, none of whose authors had yet been definitively identified. The Concord was estimated to have between forty and seventy active operatives, a small number by historical standards but disproportionately dangerous given the apparent quality of their training and the depth of their ideological commitment. They were known to maintain safe houses across the British Isles, in continental Europe, and—according to one less reliable report—in northern Morocco.

The rival organisation that the Aurors most often considered alongside the Concord, the Greywyck Order, was not in fact a hostile body. The Greywyck Order was a small private association of magical historians, founded in 1934 and based in Edinburgh, that had occasionally provided the Ministry with research assistance on questions of magical antiquity. The Order had cooperated extensively with the Auror Office during the Vorlund investigation, and its archivist, Madrigal Thrush, had become one of Potter's most valuable informal advisors. The Order's interest in pre-modern magical doctrine had given it an unusual familiarity with the kinds of sources that Vorlund himself was believed to consult, although the Order's own scholarship was conducted under the strict ethical guidelines that Vorlund's faction had so conspicuously abandoned.

## Section 4: The Press, the Public, and the Politics of Pursuit

The Wizarding public's awareness of the Vorlund investigation in the autumn of 2026 was partial, fragmentary, and in many cases incorrect. The Ministry's Office of Magical Communications, under the direction of the patient and meticulous Pomona Larkfeather, had pursued a strategy of selective disclosure throughout the case's development. The reasoning behind this strategy was uncontroversial within the Ministry. Vorlund had demonstrated, on multiple occasions, that he adjusted his operational behaviour in response to public reporting. To name him in the press was to teach him; to characterise his methods was to invite him to abandon them; to describe his suspected location was to ensure that the location was vacated within the day.

The Daily Prophet, which had spent the better part of the previous decade rebuilding its credibility under the editorship of Penelope Vane, had cooperated with the Ministry's strategy more readily than its historical reputation would have suggested. Vane herself had agreed, in a private meeting with Minister Granger-Weasley in February 2026, to a set of voluntary publication guidelines that limited the paper's coverage of ongoing high-priority investigations. The guidelines were not legally binding and could not have been enforced if they had been; the Prophet's adherence to them was a matter of editorial choice. That choice had been criticised, most vigorously by the smaller and more aggressive periodical The Wand and Quill, as a betrayal of journalistic independence. Vane had defended the arrangement in a published editorial in April that had become, within the Wizarding press community, a much-discussed statement of principle.

The Prophet's principal reporter on the Vorlund Case was Wilmot Trask, a thirty-eight-year-old correspondent with seventeen years of experience covering the Auror Office. Trask had developed, over those years, an unusually constructive working relationship with several Senior Aurors, and his coverage was widely regarded as informed without being indiscreet. His piece in the eighth of October 2026 edition, headlined "The Slow Net Closes: Inside the Most Patient Investigation of Our Time," had been widely read but had carefully omitted any reference to specific operational details. Trask's article had instead focused on the broader institutional culture of the modern Auror Office, the experience and discipline of its senior officers, and the public's rightful interest in the outcome of cases it could not be told about in detail. The article had been praised by Granger-Weasley in a private note to Vane and had been described, less generously, by The Wand and Quill as "a Ministry press release in narrative form."

Public opinion, insofar as the Ministry's polling apparatus could measure it, remained broadly supportive of the Auror Office and broadly anxious about the unspecified threat that the Office was understood to be pursuing. Polls conducted by the Department of Magical Communications in September 2026 had found that seventy-three percent of British witches and wizards retained "high confidence" in the Auror Office's competence, the highest such figure recorded since 2008. The same polls had found, more troublingly, that fifty-eight percent believed the Wizarding world to be facing "a serious magical threat that has not yet been adequately explained to the public." This combination of confidence and unease was, in the view of the Ministry's communications strategists, the best outcome that could realistically be hoped for.

The Wizengamot had taken an active interest in the case throughout 2026. The High Court's Investigative Subcommittee, chaired by the former Chief Witch Aurora Brindlebloom, had held three closed-session briefings on the investigation between June and September. The briefings had been largely supportive of the Auror Office's approach, although Brindlebloom had pressed, with the polite persistence for which she was known, for clearer benchmarks of progress. The Subcommittee's published quarterly report, which contained no operational details, had affirmed the Ministry's strategy and had recommended additional resources for the Threat Assessment Bureau.

There had nevertheless been political opposition. The Pureblood Heritage Caucus, a small but vocal grouping within the Wizengamot, had argued that the resources being expended on the Vorlund Case were disproportionate to the demonstrable threat. Their spokesperson, Lord Reginald Pendrith—whose ancestral seat at Pendrith Manor had become a frequent venue for the Caucus's gatherings—had published a series of opinion pieces in The Wand and Quill arguing that the Ministry was either misjudging the threat or, worse, manufacturing it for political purposes. Pendrith Manor itself had drawn brief Auror attention in 2024 when an unrelated investigation into the trade of mid-period German alchemical instruments had touched on a transaction involving one of Pendrith's distant cousins, but no further action had been taken and the Caucus had treated even this routine inquiry as evidence of Ministry overreach.

Beyond the immediate political theatre, the public's preoccupations in the autumn of 2026 were largely those of any ordinary year. The Quidditch League's autumn fixtures were proceeding to general satisfaction. The Wizengamot's debates over the proposed reform of the Statute of Secrecy were generating their usual heat. The Vorlund Case, for most ordinary witches and wizards, was a distant concern, an investigation conducted somewhere else, by people whose names they did not need to know. That this was precisely the impression the Ministry wished to maintain was, of course, not widely appreciated.

## Section 5: The Concord's Operations and the Discovery of Skarrowmoor Hollow

The Obsidian Concord's operational tempo throughout 2026 had been, by the standards of historical dark Wizarding movements, almost perversely restrained. Where the Death Eaters of the previous era had cultivated terror through visible atrocity, the Concord had cultivated invisibility. Its operations were small, narrowly targeted, and almost always concluded before the Ministry had become aware they were underway. The Auror Office had documented thirty-one confirmed Concord operations between January and September 2026. Twenty-four of these had been the acquisition of magical artefacts of considerable antiquity. Five had involved the abduction or coercion of individuals possessing specialised knowledge. Two had been killings—both of them figures within the European magical antiquities trade who had, in the Ministry's assessment, refused to cooperate with the Concord's procurement programme.

The breakthrough in identifying the Concord's principal headquarters had come in August 2026, through a combination of patient investigative work and an unanticipated piece of fortune. The patient work had been performed by a small analytical team within the Threat Assessment Bureau under the direction of Senior Analyst Whitlow Esterbrook. Esterbrook's team had spent the better part of nine months reconstructing the Concord's logistical patterns from a deliberately fragmented body of evidence: ledger entries from cooperative artefact dealers, confiscated correspondence, anomalies in Floo Network usage, and the geographical distribution of confirmed Concord incidents. By June 2026, the team had narrowed the likely region of the Concord's principal base to the high moorlands of the Anglo-Scottish border country, a conclusion that Esterbrook had presented to Potter with characteristic understatement as "probably correct, but I would not stake my career on it." Earlier intelligence had pointed, incorrectly, towards a long-abandoned Welsh slate quarry that had ultimately proved to have housed nothing more sinister than three feral hippogriffs.

The unanticipated fortune had come in the form of a witness. On the eleventh of August 2026, a retired Muggle hill-walker named Edmund Pickering had presented himself at the Memory Modification Office in Carlisle in a state of considerable distress. Pickering had insisted, with a coherence and consistency that the duty officer had found unusually compelling, that he had become lost on the moors above the village of Ravensteel and had stumbled upon a structure that should not have existed: a manor house of considerable size, surrounded by what he had described as "iron gates with strange symbols and the most peculiar feeling of being watched by the air itself." The Memory Modification Office had referred the case to the Auror Office as a matter of procedure, and Potter, by then alert to any unusual reports from the relevant region, had personally conducted Pickering's debriefing.

The location Pickering described was Skarrowmoor Hollow. The Auror Office had known the name, in the sense that it appeared in one nineteenth-century geographical survey of the border moors, but had no record of any magical structure at the location. A discreet aerial survey conducted on the fourteenth of August by two Aurors mounted on Thestrals had confirmed that the conventional Muggle maps showed nothing at the indicated coordinates and that conventional Wizarding records were similarly silent. A more careful magical survey, conducted from a distance of three miles and employing the Sentinel Protocols developed in 2023 for precisely this kind of work, had detected the unmistakable signature of substantial concealment magic and had identified Skarrowmoor Hollow as the operational headquarters of The Obsidian Concord with what the survey team had assessed as ninety-four percent confidence.

The discovery was both the most significant operational breakthrough of the investigation and the most delicate. Skarrowmoor Hollow was not, in any straightforward sense, a target that could simply be raided. The Sentinel Protocol surveys had detected layers of concealment magic that suggested defensive preparations of a sophistication well beyond the contemporary norm. The terrain around the Hollow offered no convenient avenues of approach. Most importantly, the Concord's known willingness to abandon and destroy compromised positions meant that any premature movement against the Hollow would almost certainly result in the loss of the very intelligence the raid was meant to secure.

Potter had therefore ordered a programme of extended observation rather than immediate action. From late August through the end of September 2026, a small surveillance team operated from a concealed position approximately seven miles from the Hollow. The team documented the comings and goings of approximately thirty-one distinct individuals, identified twelve of them as known or suspected Concord operatives, and confirmed that the Concord's principal authority figure was resident at the Hollow on at least eleven occasions during the observation period. The team also documented certain logistical patterns—the regular delivery of materials by means that suggested the involvement of cooperative goblin couriers, the periodic departure and return of small operational groups, and the unusual quietude that prevailed in the immediate vicinity of the Hollow itself.

It was during this period that the operational planning for the eventual raid began. The plan, given the codename Operation Nightthorn, would consume the better part of the autumn to develop. Potter had insisted from the outset that the operation must accomplish three objectives: the apprehension of the Concord's leadership, the dismantling of its operational capacity, and the recovery of whatever materials the Concord had accumulated at the Hollow. He had not yet known what those materials might prove to be.

## Section 6: The Aurors of Operation Nightthorn

The team assembled for Operation Nightthorn was the largest and most carefully selected force the Auror Office had committed to a single operation since 2002. Potter had been given authority over the operation's composition, an unusual departure from the standard practice of Departmental committee selection, on the grounds that the Vorlund Case had become sufficiently distinct in character that conventional procedures were poorly suited to it. He had used the authority sparingly. The operational team consisted of fourteen Aurors, six Hit Wizards, four Magical Reversal Squad specialists, and three external advisors whose participation had required the personal authorisation of the Minister herself.

Potter's principal field partner throughout the operation's planning phase was Caspian Thorne, a thirty-one-year-old Auror who had qualified in 2018 and had served alongside Potter on the Vorlund Case since the autumn of 2025. Thorne was the son of two Healers and had brought to the Auror Office an unusual combination of analytical intelligence and physical composure under pressure. He had distinguished himself in the Brackenford Mill incident of June 2026, where his quick intervention had probably prevented Potter's injuries from becoming serious, and he had been quietly identified within the Office as a future leader of his generation. Potter had requested Thorne's assignment to Operation Nightthorn on the second of September; the request had been approved without comment within an hour. A competing internal candidate, Senior Auror Dempster Halliwell, had subsequently complained at some length about the selection but had been overruled by Hartshorn personally.

The operation's tactical design drew heavily on the work of the Auror Office's Counter-Ritualistic Division, established in 2024 in response to the kinds of threats that Vorlund and the Concord were known to embody. The Division had developed what its instructors called the Sentinel Protocols, a set of procedures for engaging adversaries whose magical practice involved sustained ritual rather than the rapid duelling magic that had been the focus of Auror training for most of the preceding century. The Sentinel Protocols emphasised positional discipline, the systematic disruption of ritual focal points, and the use of specialised wand modifications to detect and counter the particular kinds of magic involved in long-form ritual practice. Senior Instructor Elspeth Marrowbye, who had overseen the Protocols' development, had personally briefed the operation's senior officers on the eighteenth of October.

The specialised wand modifications used in the operation had been provided by Olwen Mistral, the wandmaker whose small workshop in Diagon Alley had become, since the retirement of Garrick Ollivander in 2019, the principal source of Auror-grade equipment in Britain. Mistral, fifty-nine years old and possessed of a temperament that her customers had variously described as exacting, mercurial, and entirely without small talk, had developed for the Auror Office a series of modifications that allowed standard wands to detect the magical resonance characteristic of pre-modern ritual practice. The modifications had been provided to all members of the operational team on the first of November 2026, accompanied by Mistral's brief and characteristic note: "These will fail if you mistreat them. Try not to." A competing wandmaker in Hogsmeade, Cornelius Brell, had submitted a rival proposal earlier in the year that had been politely declined on the grounds that his prototypes had repeatedly failed laboratory testing.

The three external advisors authorised by the Minister had been included over the moderate objection of certain senior Departmental officials who had viewed any non-Auror involvement in such an operation as a procedural irregularity. Potter had argued for their inclusion on grounds of substantive necessity, and the Minister had agreed. The first of the advisors was Madrigal Thrush, an archivist whose detailed knowledge of pre-modern magical doctrine was considered essential for the contextual interpretation of any materials recovered at the Hollow. The second was Alaric Pendraig, a retired Curse-Breaker whose three decades of work for Gringotts had given him unusual experience with the kinds of defensive enchantments the Concord was likely to have employed. The third was a witch identified in the operational documentation only as "Consultant V," whose identity remained classified at the highest Ministerial level and whose participation had been negotiated through intermediaries whose names were not recorded.

The operational planning was complicated, as such planning always was, by the gap between intelligence and certainty. The surveillance teams had developed a detailed picture of activity around the Hollow, but its interior remained unknown. Potter had refused several proposals for closer reconnaissance on the grounds that detection at the planning stage would be operationally catastrophic. The plan therefore had to accommodate substantial uncertainty about the Hollow's internal layout, the disposition of its defenders, and the location and nature of the Concord's principal materials. Contingencies were developed for all of the most plausible internal configurations. Drills were conducted, in the third week of October, on a magically constructed approximation of the Hollow's likely structure at a Ministry training facility in Salisbury Plain.

The operation's launch date was set for the seventh of November 2026, a date chosen primarily for the lunar conditions and the anticipated weather over the border moors. Potter had spent the final days before the operation in an unusual quietude. Thorne, who had observed his partner at close range throughout the planning, had described Potter's mood in his subsequent operational debrief as "entirely calm, in the way that only people with no remaining illusions are calm."

## Section 7: Skarrowmoor Hollow and the Discovery in Its Catacombs

Operation Nightthorn began at 4:17 in the morning of the seventh of November 2026 and concluded, in its principal operational phase, at 11:42 the same day. The Auror force approached Skarrowmoor Hollow from three directions across the moor, taking advantage of a heavy cold mist that the Magical Weather Forecasting Office had identified, with considerable satisfaction in its operational utility, three days in advance. The Hollow's outer defences—a layered system of warning enchantments, a perimeter of compulsion-based deterrents, and three concealed apparition wards—were dismantled or bypassed by the Magical Reversal Squad over the course of approximately ninety minutes. The Concord's defenders, taken almost entirely by surprise despite the sophistication of their preparations, mounted resistance at twelve identified positions within and around the manor itself.

Skarrowmoor Hollow was, by the eventual reckoning of the Auror Office's surveyors, a structure of considerable antiquity. Its principal building was a manor house dating in its earliest sections to the early fifteenth century, with substantial expansions in the seventeenth and nineteenth centuries. The manor was constructed in the dark grey stone characteristic of the border country, surrounded by a perimeter wall of similar stone, and approached by a single narrow track that wound up from the moor below. The structure had been concealed from Muggle awareness, and from most casual Wizarding awareness, by enchantments whose age and depth suggested that the concealment had been continuously maintained for at least three centuries. The Hollow had not, in other words, been a recent acquisition. Its current occupants had taken possession of a structure that had already been substantially prepared for the kind of work they wished to do.

The interior of the Hollow, when the Auror force had finally penetrated to it, presented a tableau that Potter would later describe in his official report as "more library than fortress." The principal living quarters were spare to the point of austerity. The kitchens and dining halls suggested a community of perhaps thirty resident operatives. The main public rooms had been converted into reading rooms, study halls, and what appeared to be a substantial scriptorium. The walls were lined with manuscripts and printed volumes, many of them of considerable rarity, and the cataloguing arrangements suggested a research institution of more than incidental seriousness. Madrigal Thrush, who had entered the Hollow with the second wave of Aurors, had been observed by several witnesses to stand in stillness for several minutes in the principal reading room, evidently overwhelmed by the scale and quality of the collection.

The defenders had numbered, by the final tally, twenty-eight active combatants. Of these, nineteen were captured, six were killed in the course of the operation, and three escaped through means that the post-operational review was unable fully to reconstruct. The Concord's principal authority figure was not among the captured, the killed, or the certainly identified escapees. His personal quarters in the western wing of the manor had been recently occupied—papers on his writing desk had been actively in use within hours of the raid—but he himself had evidently departed before the operation's commencement. The question of how he had known to depart, and when, and through what channel he had received warning, would dominate the post-operational investigations for months.

The operation's most consequential discovery, and the one that would define its long historical significance, occurred at 9:23 in the morning, when a Magical Reversal Squad team led by Specialist Renata Ó Faoláin penetrated the catacombs that lay beneath the manor's eastern wing. The catacombs were of substantially greater antiquity than the manor above them, dating—as later analysis would establish—to the late twelfth century, and bearing the unmistakable marks of a religious or ritual purpose long predating any documented Wizarding occupation of the site. Ó Faoláin's team navigated approximately three hundred yards of corridor and chamber before arriving at a sealed doorway marked with the same circular eclipse sigil that had appeared on the parchment fragment Potter had recovered in Inverness two years earlier.

Behind the doorway, in a chamber whose dimensions and acoustics the survey team would subsequently describe as "designed for the containment of something whose nature we are still attempting to characterise," they found The Threnody Stone.

The Threnody Stone was, at first inspection, a roughly spherical object of dark crystalline material, approximately twenty-three centimetres in diameter, mounted on a low pedestal of black marble. It had been the subject of at least eleven written references in the Concord's recovered correspondence, none of which had previously been intelligible to the Auror Office's analysts. Madrigal Thrush, summoned to the catacombs at Potter's immediate request, had identified the object within seconds and had become, for the only time in the operation's documented record, visibly shaken. "This is not supposed to exist," she had said. "It was destroyed in 1719. There are seven separate witnesses to its destruction." Potter had observed that it appeared, against all contrary testimony, to be sitting before them. Thrush had agreed that this was indeed the case.

The Stone was secured under the highest available containment protocols. It was removed from Skarrowmoor Hollow, under conditions of extraordinary care, on the morning of the ninth of November 2026. Early Ministry communications, in a confusion that would later require formal correction, briefly conflated the Threnody Stone with the Penumbra Crystal—a superficially similar but historically unrelated nineteenth-century object that had been recovered from Pendrith Manor in a separate operation in 2024. The two objects shared little beyond colour, approximate dimensions, and the consistency with which the Wizarding press misidentified them. The error was corrected in the Ministry's third operational bulletin and did not subsequently recur.

## Section 8: The Wizengamot, the Hearings, and the Unresolved Questions

The capture of Skarrowmoor Hollow, the recovery of the Threnody Stone, and the apprehension of nineteen senior Concord operatives would, in any earlier era of Auror operations, have been treated as an unambiguous triumph. The Wizengamot proceedings that followed the operation through November and December 2026 had treated it as something more complicated. The escape of the Concord's leader, the implications of the Stone's existence, and the questions raised by the apparent forewarning of the Hollow's principal occupant had cast the operation in a light that was both more sober and, in certain respects, more consequential than a simple operational success would have permitted.

The Wizengamot's principal proceedings had been conducted by the High Court's Investigative Subcommittee under the continued chairship of Aurora Brindlebloom, supported by a panel of six senior Wizengamot members and advised by Inspector-General Felix Underwood, whose office had been responsible for the post-operational review. Underwood, sixty-three years old and forty years into a career in magical law enforcement that had begun in the Magical Reversal Squad of the early 1980s, was widely regarded as the most rigorous internal investigator the Ministry had produced in his generation. His presence at the Subcommittee's proceedings had ensured that the Aurors' testimony would be tested with a thoroughness that no political pressure could have moderated. A junior colleague of his, Deputy Inspector Hilda Marrowfax, had assisted with the day-to-day documentation but had played no substantive role in the analytical conclusions.

The principal questions before the Subcommittee were three. First: how had the Concord's leadership known to depart Skarrowmoor Hollow before the operation's commencement? Second: what was the Threnody Stone, and what did its presence at the Hollow imply about the Concord's broader purposes? Third: what should the Ministry now do, given that the principal target of the most significant Auror operation in two decades remained at large and presumably regrouping?

The first question had occupied the post-operational review for the better part of November. The review had identified four plausible mechanisms by which warning could have been received: a leak from within the operational team, a leak from within the broader Auror Office, a leak from within the Wizengamot Subcommittee that had been briefed on the operation's general parameters, or detection by means that the Ministry had not yet identified. Underwood's preliminary findings, presented to the Subcommittee on the second of December, had assessed the first two possibilities as unlikely but not impossible, the third as possible but unlikely given the limited operational detail provided to the Subcommittee, and the fourth as the most probable. The implication—that the Concord possessed surveillance or detection capabilities significantly in advance of the Ministry's own—had been received by the Subcommittee with an appropriate seriousness.

The second question had been substantially more difficult. The Threnody Stone, once securely removed to a containment facility within the Department of Mysteries, had been subjected to the most intensive scholarly examination that the Ministry had conducted of any magical artefact in living memory. The team responsible for the examination had been led by Unspeakable Hesperia Vance and had included, in an arrangement of unusual scholarly cooperation, both Madrigal Thrush of the Greywyck Order and a small panel of international consultants whose names had not been made public. Their preliminary report, transmitted to the Wizengamot Subcommittee in confidence on the eighteenth of December, had confirmed the Stone's identity beyond reasonable doubt and had begun to outline the implications of its survival.

The third question had been the principal subject of the Subcommittee's published recommendations, issued on the twenty-second of December 2026. The Subcommittee had endorsed the Auror Office's intention to continue the pursuit of the Concord's leader as the Ministry's highest operational priority. It had recommended an expansion of the Threat Assessment Bureau's analytical capacity. It had recommended the establishment of a permanent inter-departmental working group on the Concord and on related movements. It had recommended, with a delicacy appropriate to the sensitivity of the question, a review of the Ministry's internal communications security. And it had recommended that the Threnody Stone remain under the Department of Mysteries' indefinite custody pending the completion of Unspeakable Vance's full scholarly report.

The Subcommittee's published findings had been received in the Wizarding press with a mixture of relief and anxiety. The Daily Prophet had editorialised under the headline "A Hollow Won, A Wizard Lost" and had praised the operation's execution while noting, with appropriate restraint, the questions that remained unanswered. The Wand and Quill had taken a more critical line, arguing in successive editorials that the operation's incomplete success demonstrated the inadequacy of the Ministry's broader approach. Lord Pendrith, predictably, had called for the resignation of multiple senior officials and had introduced a motion of no confidence in the Department of Magical Law Enforcement that had been defeated by a substantial margin.

Within the Auror Office itself, the post-operational period was a time of careful institutional reflection. Potter had testified before the Subcommittee on three separate occasions and had answered every question that was put to him with a directness that several observers had described as exemplary. He had not, in any of his testimony, expressed regret for the operation's design. He had also not, in any of his testimony, predicted the timeline on which the Concord's leader might be located again. The honesty of the second omission had, in its way, been the most striking feature of his testimony.

## Section 9: The Threnody Stone and the Legacy of Ysolde Brackenmere

The Threnody Stone's identification, confirmed by Unspeakable Hesperia Vance's panel in early December 2026, established it as one of the most consequential magical artefacts to come into Ministry custody in modern times. The Stone's history, insofar as it could be reconstructed from the surviving documentary evidence, traced back to the late fifteenth century and to the work of one of the most enigmatic figures of pre-Reformation English magical history: the alchemist and ritual theorist Ysolde Brackenmere.

Ysolde Brackenmere had been born, by the most reliable available account, in 1454 in the county of Westmorland, in a small Wizarding household whose precise location had been lost to the cartographic ambiguities of the period. She had emerged into documentary visibility in 1471 as a junior member of the household of the elderly alchemist Hieronymus Wexcombe, under whose tutelage she had developed the foundations of the practice for which she would later become both celebrated and notorious. Wexcombe had died in 1478. Brackenmere had inherited his library, his apparatus, and a small but adequate income, and had relocated in 1479 to a property near the village of Crowsmere on the western edge of the Lake District. There, between 1479 and her death in 1502, she had conducted the work that would establish her place in the secret history of British magic.

Brackenmere's principal contribution to magical theory had been the development of what later writers had called the "resonant ritual," a class of magical operation that depended for its efficacy not on the channelled power of its practitioner but on the harmonic alignment of its physical apparatus with the magical structure of the world. The technique had been considered, even in its own time, both intellectually impressive and morally questionable. It had permitted operations that the conventional Wizarding establishment had considered the exclusive province of natural magical talent—and it had permitted them, when properly executed, with a precision and a permanence that natural talent could not reliably achieve. The institutional response to Brackenmere's work had been one of cautious suppression. Her writings had not been formally banned, but they had been gradually withdrawn from circulation, and by the early seventeenth century her name had been almost entirely effaced from the standard alchemical histories. A near-contemporary alchemist, Tobias Wrenwood of Norwich, had occasionally been credited in later sources with discoveries that were in fact Brackenmere's; the attribution error had been corrected only in the late nineteenth century by the Cambridge magical historian Sebastian Quail.

The Threnody Stone had been Brackenmere's most ambitious construction. The Stone had been built—the verb is correct, in the sense that it was a constructed object rather than a discovered one—between 1493 and 1497, drawing on materials whose provenance Brackenmere had documented in her surviving notebooks with an unusual attention to detail. Its purpose, as Brackenmere herself had described it in the third of her four extant treatises, had been to permit the resonant ritualist to perform what she had termed "operations of memory upon the magical record of place." The Stone had been designed, in less guarded language, to allow its possessor to access and to alter the magical traces left by previous events at any location to which the Stone could be brought into proximity. The implications of such a capability—both for the historical record and for the future security of any magical site—had not been lost on Brackenmere's contemporaries, several of whom had argued, with what hindsight suggested was inadequate vigour, that the Stone's construction should be prevented.

Brackenmere had completed the Stone in 1497 and had used it, by her own account, on at least eleven occasions before her death. The nature of those uses had been described in her notebooks with a deliberate vagueness that her successors had been unable to penetrate. After her death in 1502, the Stone had passed through a sequence of private custodians whose identities had been only partially documented. It had surfaced in the historical record on three subsequent occasions—in 1567, in 1641, and most recently in 1719—and had on each occasion been the subject of efforts at its destruction that had been believed, at the time, to be successful. The 1719 destruction, conducted by a coalition of European magical authorities at a site in the Low Countries, had been particularly thorough. The destruction had been witnessed by seven independent observers whose accounts had been preserved in the relevant archives. The Stone, in the consensus understanding of every magical scholar who had subsequently considered the question, had ceased to exist on the eleventh of August 1719.

That this consensus had been incorrect was now established. The Stone recovered at Skarrowmoor Hollow had been examined against the surviving documentary descriptions and had matched in every material particular. Vance's panel had concluded, with a confidence that they had described as approaching but not quite reaching certainty, that the Stone was either Brackenmere's original or an exact reconstruction conducted from her original notebooks by a practitioner of comparable skill. The first possibility was disturbing. The second was, in its different way, more disturbing still. The Concord's possession of the Stone, regardless of how it had come into their hands, indicated that its leadership's interest in the deep history of pre-Reformation magic was not merely scholarly. They had acquired, or constructed, an instrument capable of altering the magical record of the past. The question of how they intended to use it remained, at the year's end, the principal mystery before the Ministry.

## Section 10: Honors, Reflections, and the Continuing Pursuit

The Auror Office's recognition of Operation Nightthorn took the form, in the closing weeks of 2026, of the Ministry's most prestigious institutional honours. Senior Auror Harry Potter was awarded the Veritas Compendium on the seventeenth of December 2026, the first recipient of the award in eleven years and only the fourteenth in the award's seventy-two-year history. The Veritas Compendium was given for "service to the Wizarding community of a character that no anticipated honour could have foreseen," a formulation whose deliberate imprecision had been the subject of academic commentary for as long as the award had existed. The presentation ceremony was held in the Atrium of the Ministry of Magic. Minister Granger-Weasley delivered the principal address, in which she described Potter's career as one that had "made the impossible look like procedure, and the necessary look like duty." Potter accepted the award with a brief and characteristically modest response in which he had named, by way of credit, every member of the Operation Nightthorn team and several individuals whose contributions to the Vorlund Case had not been previously made public.

Auror Caspian Thorne received the Order of Merlin, Third Class, at the same ceremony. Specialist Renata Ó Faoláin, whose team had recovered the Threnody Stone, received the Magical Reversal Squad's Distinguished Service Cross, an honour that her own service had not previously bestowed and that had been authorised specifically for the occasion. Madrigal Thrush received an honorary Ministry Citation, the first such honour in the Greywyck Order's ninety-two-year history. Olwen Mistral, characteristically, had declined to accept any formal recognition, though she had agreed to allow her workshop to be photographed for the Daily Prophet's coverage of the ceremony, an unprecedented concession that several of her long-standing customers had found alarming. The Wand and Quill, having spent most of November criticising the operation, had spent most of December criticising the awards.

The institutional reflection that followed the operation's recognition had been more substantial than the celebratory rhetoric might have suggested. The Auror Office, under the continued direction of Senior Auror Galenwise Hartshorn, had initiated a comprehensive review of its operational doctrine in light of the Vorlund Case's implications. The review's preliminary findings, circulated in confidence in early January 2027, had identified six areas in which the Office's existing capabilities were considered inadequate to the kinds of threats represented by the Obsidian Concord. These included its capacity for sustained surveillance of high-coherence threats, its ability to interpret pre-modern magical material, its operational security against advanced detection, its coordination with private specialists, its understanding of artefact-driven adversaries, and its strategic patience under political pressure. Each of these areas had become the subject of dedicated working groups whose reports were expected over the course of 2027.

The continuing pursuit of Malachar Vorlund himself had been Potter's principal preoccupation in the weeks following the operation. The Auror Office had no reliable intelligence on Vorlund's location after his departure from Skarrowmoor Hollow. The absence of any subsequent Concord operations through December 2026 suggested that the organisation had been more substantially disrupted than its leadership might have hoped, but it also suggested that Vorlund himself was operating with a discipline that would make his rediscovery exceptionally difficult. Potter's working assumption, expressed privately to Caspian Thorne and to a small number of senior colleagues, was that Vorlund would resurface within eighteen to thirty months and that the form of his resurfacing would be calculated to inflict the maximum possible damage on the Ministry's operational confidence. The assumption was not optimistic, but neither was it despairing. It was, in Potter's characteristic mode, simply realistic.

The Threnody Stone remained, at the year's end, in the secure custody of the Department of Mysteries. Unspeakable Hesperia Vance's full scholarly report on the Stone was expected in the late spring of 2027. Preliminary indications suggested that the report would recommend the Stone's permanent containment under conditions exceeding even those applied to the most sensitive items in the Department's existing holdings. The question of whether the Stone could safely be destroyed—the question that the magical authorities of 1719 had believed they had answered conclusively—would be considered separately by a panel whose composition had not yet been determined. Madrigal Thrush had argued, in her informal correspondence with Potter, that the Stone's destruction should not be attempted until the deeper purposes of its construction had been more fully understood. "Brackenmere built nothing carelessly," Thrush had written. "We should be slow to destroy what we do not yet understand."

The Wizarding world entered 2027 with the Vorlund Case still open. The Concord had been dispersed but not eliminated. The Stone had been recovered but not neutralised. Malachar Vorlund himself remained at large, somewhere in the wide and patient world that he had spent the previous decade learning to inhabit unnoticed. Harry Potter, at the close of his most demanding professional year, returned quietly to the work that the Veritas Compendium had recognised: the steady, unspectacular, indispensable work of pursuing what the Wizarding community required to have pursued. He was forty-six years old. He intended to continue.

qa_pairs.question: Who originally created the artifact recovered from the headquarters of the organization founded by the dark wizard Harry Potter is investigating?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 1 establishes Harry Potter is investigating Malachar Vorlund. Section 3 establishes Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord in 2018. Section 5 reveals the Concord's headquarters is at Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 7 reveals the Threnody Stone was found in Skarrowmoor Hollow's catacombs. Section 9 establishes Ysolde Brackenmere built the Threnody Stone between 1493 and 1497.

qa_pairs.question: What artifact was discovered in the catacombs of the headquarters of the organization founded by Malachar Vorlund?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 3 establishes Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord. Section 5 establishes the Concord operates from Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 7 reveals the Threnody Stone was discovered in the catacombs beneath Skarrowmoor Hollow's eastern wing.

qa_pairs.question: Who founded the organization that was using Skarrowmoor Hollow as its operational base?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 5 identifies Skarrowmoor Hollow as the operational headquarters of The Obsidian Concord. Section 3 establishes that Malachar Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord in 2018.

qa_pairs.question: Who was the mentor of the alchemist who built the artifact recovered during Operation Nightthorn?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 5 introduces Operation Nightthorn as the planned raid on Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 7 shows the Threnody Stone was recovered there during the operation. Section 9 establishes Ysolde Brackenmere built the Stone, and that she had been a junior member of the household of the elderly alchemist Hieronymus Wexcombe, under whose tutelage she developed her foundational practice.

qa_pairs.question: In what year did the alchemist who created the artifact found at Skarrowmoor Hollow die?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 7 establishes the Threnody Stone was found at Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 9 reveals that Ysolde Brackenmere created the Threnody Stone, and that she conducted her work between 1479 and her death in 1502.

qa_pairs.question: Which threat classification did the Auror Office assign to the wizard who founded the organization headquartered at Skarrowmoor Hollow?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 5 ties Skarrowmoor Hollow to The Obsidian Concord. Section 3 establishes Vorlund founded the Concord. Section 1 reveals that by July 2026, the Auror Office had formally designated Malachar Vorlund as a Class A Magical Threat.

qa_pairs.question: Which wandmaker provided the wand modifications used by the Aurors in the operation that raided the headquarters of The Obsidian Concord?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 5 establishes Skarrowmoor Hollow as the Concord's headquarters and introduces Operation Nightthorn as the raid plan. Section 6 reveals that the specialised wand modifications used in the operation had been provided by Olwen Mistral.

qa_pairs.question: Which order's archivist served as an external advisor on the operation that recovered the Threnody Stone?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 7 establishes the Threnody Stone was recovered during Operation Nightthorn. Section 6 lists Madrigal Thrush as an external advisor on the operation. Section 3 establishes that Madrigal Thrush is the archivist of The Greywyck Order.

qa_pairs.question: In what year was the alchemist born who originally constructed the artifact found in the catacombs of the Obsidian Concord's headquarters?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 5 establishes the Concord's headquarters at Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 7 reveals the Threnody Stone was found in its catacombs. Section 9 reveals that Ysolde Brackenmere built the Stone and had been born, by the most reliable available account, in 1454 in Westmorland.

qa_pairs.question: Who received the Order of Merlin Third Class at the same ceremony where the Auror investigating the founder of The Obsidian Concord was honoured?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 3 establishes Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord. Section 1 establishes Harry Potter was investigating Vorlund. Section 10 reveals Potter was awarded the Veritas Compendium and that Caspian Thorne received the Order of Merlin, Third Class, at the same ceremony.

qa_pairs.question: Which Inspector-General oversaw the post-operational review of the raid that recovered the Threnody Stone?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 7 establishes the Threnody Stone was recovered during Operation Nightthorn. Section 8 reveals that the Wizengamot Investigative Subcommittee proceedings on the operation were advised by Inspector-General Felix Underwood, whose office had been responsible for the post-operational review.

qa_pairs.question: Which journalist was the principal correspondent covering the case involving the wizard who founded The Obsidian Concord?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 3 establishes Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord. Section 1 names the investigation as the Vorlund Case. Section 4 reveals that the Daily Prophet's principal reporter on the Vorlund Case was Wilmot Trask.

qa_pairs.question: In what location is the artifact created by Ysolde Brackenmere said to have been rediscovered in November 2026?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 9 establishes that Ysolde Brackenmere created the Threnody Stone. Section 7 establishes that the Threnody Stone was discovered in the catacombs beneath Skarrowmoor Hollow during Operation Nightthorn.

qa_pairs.question: Which Unspeakable led the panel that examined the artifact recovered from the headquarters of the organization founded by Malachar Vorlund?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 3 establishes Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord. Section 5 ties the Concord to Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 7 ties the Threnody Stone's recovery to that location. Section 8 reveals that the team responsible for examining the Stone in the Department of Mysteries had been led by Unspeakable Hesperia Vance.

qa_pairs.question: Which award was given to the Auror investigating the founder of the organization that controlled Skarrowmoor Hollow?
qa_pairs.answer_justification: Section 5 establishes the Concord controlled Skarrowmoor Hollow. Section 3 establishes Malachar Vorlund founded The Obsidian Concord. Section 1 establishes Harry Potter was investigating Vorlund. Section 10 reveals that Potter was awarded the Veritas Compendium on 17 December 2026.
